summary: The frontend container is an Angular‑based, component‑driven UI that implements presentation, service, and data‑access layers using Angular, TypeScript, RxJS, Angular Material and related tooling. It supports a rich set of deed‑and‑document management capabilities—including authentication, CRUD operations, import, workflow control, digital signatures, reporting, PDF rendering and background job handling—through integration with multiple REST APIs. While the architecture follows a moderate separation of concerns, the codebase shows significant technical debt: a very high number of services, components, adapters and pipes, shallow module boundaries, and missing façade abstractions, resulting in moderate coupling and cohesion and a grade of C.

layers: Service / Business Logic (Services, Guards, Interceptors, Resolvers)
Data Access / Integration (Adapters, API Services, HTTP interfaces)

capabilities: Create, edit, view, and delete deed entries
Manage deed workflows and status transitions
Search and filter deeds, participants, and connections
Generate and export reports and metadata
Handle document metadata and PDF rendering
Execute background jobs and reencryption tasks
Interact with external REST APIs for deed registry, document metadata, actions, jobs, and tasks
Apply rate limiting, error handling, and busy indicators
functional.bounded_context: Deed & Document Management (Land Registry / Property Transfer)

debt_indicators: Excessive number of services (131) – likely God services or duplicated responsibilities
Very large component count (128) – many UI components may be thin wrappers over services, indicating weak UI‑logic separation
High number of adapters (50) – potential overlap in data‑access abstraction
Over‑use of pipes (67) – many tiny transformations that could be consolidated
Modules appear shallow (16) – feature slicing may be insufficient, leading to cross‑module imports
Missing façade/service‑layer abstractions for external API modules
recommendations: Consolidate related services into domain‑specific façade services to reduce coupling and improve cohesion
Introduce or strengthen feature modules (e.g., Deed, Document, Workflow) to encapsulate components, services, and pipes per bounded context
Audit adapters for duplicated logic; merge similar adapters and define clear interface contracts
Refactor overly granular pipes into shared utility functions or combine related pipes
Apply the Single Responsibility Principle to large components and services – split when they handle multiple concerns
Document and enforce module dependency rules to avoid circular imports and tighter coupling

overall_assessment: The frontend exhibits a solid functional baseline and uses appropriate Angular technologies, but architectural fragmentation and surplus artifacts have caused moderate coupling and reduced cohesion, earning it a C grade. Targeted refactoring and modularization are required to improve maintainability and scalability.
top_recommendations: Consolidate services into domain‑specific façade layers to lower coupling and increase cohesion.
Restructure the codebase into well‑defined feature modules aligned with the bounded contexts (Deed, Document, Workflow, etc.).
Reduce redundancy by auditing and merging duplicated adapters and over‑granular pipes, replacing them with shared utilities.

layers: Service / Business Logic (Services, Guards, Interceptors, Resolvers)
Data Access / Integration (Adapters, API Services, HTTP interfaces)
